gloss: Pay for the City's workforce — police officers, nurses, Muni operators, librarians and everyone else on the payroll.
The employer's share of employee benefits the City is required to fund: pension contributions, health coverage, Social Security and Medicare.
Services the City buys rather than staffs: professional services, maintenance contracts, utilities, rent, training, travel.
Grants the City makes to outside organizations — mostly nonprofits delivering health, housing and social services.
Internal billing: one City department paying another for its work (legal counsel, fleet, IT, real estate). Money that stays inside the City.
Principal and interest payments on bonds and other borrowing.
Accounting transfers between accounts inside the same fund — internal money movement, not services delivered. Cancelled citywide by the transfer adjustment.
Money moved from one City fund to another (for example General Fund support to Muni). Cancelled citywide by the transfer adjustment.
Purchases of physical assets: equipment, vehicles, building improvements, infrastructure.
Direct assistance paid to or on behalf of individuals — public-assistance grants such as CalWORKs and county adult assistance.
In-kind and support assistance tied to public-aid programs, alongside direct aid payments.
Physical goods the City consumes: pharmaceuticals, fuel, food, office and maintenance supplies.
Budget set aside for defined projects and initiatives, spent through the programs that carry them out.
Revenue budgeted but deliberately not appropriated for spending — retained as fund balance or reserve.
Revenue set aside for a designated future purpose rather than appropriated for spending this year.
Revenue routed to reserves required by the City Charter (rainy-day style reserves).
Care purchased for people the City is responsible for: foster care, board-and-care, shelter beds, treatment placements.
Upkeep of City buildings and facilities: repairs, renovation and maintenance projects.
Benefit payments made from fiduciary funds the City administers (retirement and health trusts).
Accounting entries that are not cash spending (non-expenditure items).
Spending recorded as capital assets on the balance sheet rather than as an operating expense.
Refunds or distributions of contributions, mainly retirement-system flows.
General Fund subsidy transferred to departments whose own revenues do not cover their costs.
Transfers out earmarked for capital spending in another fund.
Other financing outflows, such as bond refundings and similar financing transactions.
Budget authority carried forward from a prior year — a budgeting entry, not new money.
Overhead charged out to other departments and funds; appears negative where a department recovers its overhead costs from the departments it serves.
The negative adjustment that cancels inter- and intra-fund transfers so the citywide total counts each dollar once.
Legacy placeholder for General Fund support (zero-value skeleton rows from the FY2018 system migration).

Fees charged for services — dominated by the City's enterprises billing their customers: hospital care billed to insurers and Medi-Cal, airport charges to airlines, water and sewer bills. Not primarily fees on residents.
The City's share of the 1% property tax on assessed value, plus voter-approved add-ons.
Taxes on business activity: gross receipts tax, homelessness gross receipts tax, business registration fees.
Local taxes other than property and business taxes: the City's sales-tax share, hotel tax, real-estate transfer tax, parking tax, utility users tax.
Money from the State of California: health and social-services realignment funds and program reimbursements.
Federal money: Medicaid and Medicare reimbursements plus federal grants for housing, transit and emergencies.
Money from other governments — regional agencies, other counties and special districts.
Rent and concession income from City property: airport concessions, port leases, facility rentals.
Earnings on the City's cash and investment pools.
Parking and traffic fines, court penalties, code-enforcement fines.
Business licenses, construction and use permits, franchise fees from utilities.
Employee and employer contributions flowing into the retirement and health-benefit systems.
Revenue that fits no other category — settlements, donations, miscellaneous income.
Financing inflows: bond and loan proceeds, proceeds from asset sales.
Prior-year fund balance brought into the budget as a source of funding.
Money received from another City fund — the receiving side of fund-to-fund transfers. Cancelled citywide by the transfer adjustment.
Transfers in earmarked for capital spending.
Capital contributed by outside parties toward City assets.
One-off extraordinary accounting gains.
Technical accounts from the financial-system conversion (zero-value skeleton rows).
Accounting transfers between accounts inside the same fund — internal mechanics, not money coming in from outside.
One department recovering its costs from another — internal billing income, the mirror of 'Services of Other Depts' on the spending side.
The negative adjustment on the revenue side that cancels inter- and intra-fund transfers so the citywide total counts each dollar once.
